nodes.label: Unattended overnight execution
nodes.description: The pipeline must run from approximately 2am to completion without any human interaction.

nodes.label: Full price history per product
nodes.description: Every price observation must be persisted with a timestamp so the complete history is queryable over time.

nodes.label: Alert on price drop below threshold
nodes.description: When a tracked product's price falls below a user-configured threshold, an alert must be triggered automatically.

nodes.label: Cross-run deduplication
nodes.description: Scraping the same product in consecutive runs must not produce duplicate records in the database.

nodes.label: Auth-protected platform support
nodes.description: The scraper must handle Amazon and Walmart, both of which require an authenticated session to return full product data.

nodes.label: Bot-detection resilience
nodes.description: The scraper must apply stealth measures sufficient to avoid trivial bot-detection on all three target platforms.

nodes.label: Per-product error isolation
nodes.description: A failure to scrape one product must not abort the pipeline; the error is logged and the run continues.

nodes.label: Offline scraping tests
nodes.description: Scraping and extraction logic must be testable against saved HTML fixtures without making live HTTP requests.

nodes.label: Python 3.11+ runtime
nodes.description: All pipeline code is written in Python 3.11+ running on Ubuntu 22.04.
nodes.rationale: User is most comfortable with Python; no competing requirement justifies a different language.

nodes.label: Playwright for browser automation
nodes.description: Use Playwright (Python bindings) with playwright-stealth for all scraping, including auth-protected sites.
nodes.rationale: Better async support, network interception API for fixture injection, and first-class stealth plugin compared to Selenium. Requests+BS4 ruled out because Amazon/Walmart render prices via JavaScript.

nodes.label: SQLite for persistence
nodes.description: Use SQLite (WAL mode) as the database. Accessed via the sqlite3 stdlib module wrapped in a thin helper layer.
nodes.rationale: Zero infrastructure overhead for a personal 50-product use case on a VPS. PostgreSQL is unnecessary complexity.

nodes.label: Gmail SMTP for alerts
nodes.description: Send alert emails via smtplib using a Gmail app password stored in .env.
nodes.rationale: User already has a Gmail account and wants zero additional cost. No Twilio or third-party API.

nodes.label: APScheduler for overnight scheduling
nodes.description: Use APScheduler BackgroundScheduler with a cron trigger to fire the pipeline at 2am daily.
nodes.rationale: Python-native scheduling keeps error handling within the same process. Avoids cron + shell script glue that loses Python exceptions.

nodes.label: Cookie-file authentication
nodes.description: User exports browser cookies to a Netscape-format file; Playwright loads them at session launch for Amazon and Walmart.
nodes.rationale: Avoids storing plaintext credentials. Compatible with browser extensions like Cookie-Editor. Session is valid without re-login for 24-48h.

nodes.label: Product URLs remain stable
nodes.description: Assumes that product page URLs (canonical Amazon ASIN URL, eBay item URL, Walmart item URL) do not change or redirect unexpectedly between runs.

nodes.label: Price is present in rendered DOM
nodes.description: Assumes that after JavaScript rendering, the product price appears as text in the DOM and is reachable by a CSS selector.

nodes.label: Exported cookies stay valid 24-48h
nodes.description: Assumes that cookies exported from a logged-in browser session remain valid for at least one pipeline cycle (24h), so no mid-run re-authentication is needed.

nodes.label: Bot-detection rules change slowly
nodes.description: Assumes that the fingerprinting and bot-detection logic on Amazon, eBay, and Walmart does not change more frequently than once per week, giving time to patch selectors.

nodes.label: Gmail SMTP available at 2am
nodes.description: Assumes Gmail SMTP is reachable from the VPS at the scheduled run time and that the app password does not expire without notice.

nodes.label: Bot detection breaks scraping
nodes.description: Amazon or Walmart may fingerprint the headless browser and return a CAPTCHA page or empty product data instead of a price.

nodes.label: CSS selector breakage on site redesign
nodes.description: A site redesign can silently break price extraction — the scraper returns None rather than raising an exception, causing missed alerts.

nodes.label: CAPTCHA blocks unattended session
nodes.description: A CAPTCHA served mid-session has no automated bypass in this design. The run stalls or skips the affected product.
